data.title: Nasdaq, S&P waver after hitting record highs; traders bump up odds of December rate cut
data.description:
Wall Street's major equity averages were mixed Tuesday, with investors sorting through the first in a batch of labor-market data due this week after a record-setting session a day earlier.

The S&P 500 (SP500) -0.1%, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.2%, after each finished at record highs in Monday's trade. The Dow (DJI), meanwhile, shed -0.2%.

Investors early Tuesday received the October Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey, or JOLTS, report, in what will be a busy week for jobs data that should further shape rate-cut expectations. Job openings rose more than expected in October, up 7.744M versus 7.490M consensus and the downwardly revised 7.372M print for September.

"In the context of a gradually cooling labor market on the cusp of a soft landing, today’s JOLTS report is about as good as can reasonably be expected. Layoffs remained low and openings ticked up alongside quits — signs that employer and job seeker confidence, respectively, are improving," Cory Stahle, economist at Indeed Hiring Lab, said.

Traders on Tuesday pushed up the probability of the Federal Reserve cutting its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points at its upcoming meeting. Odds rose to ~74% from ~62% on Monday, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

﻿"The Fed’s December 18th decision will be a close one, but if the majority of voting members prioritize the employment mandate, markets should expect a cut in policy rates, supporting risk appetite," Jeffrey Roach, chief economist at LPL Financial, said.

A look a private hiring in November will arrive on Wednesday from ADP, the same day that Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is scheduled to speak in New York. The U.S. non-farm payrolls report for November is due Friday.

Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said Tuesday he expects interest rates to continue falling in the next year. Also speaking Tuesday, Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kugler said inflation appears on a sustainable path toward the 2% target and that she will take a "meeting by meeting" approach in making policy decisions. On Monday, Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller said he was leaning toward supporting a cut in December.

In the fixed-income market Tuesday, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) reversed course and rose 1 basis point to 4.22%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 3 basis points to 4.16%.

Back in equities, eight of the 11 sectors in the S&P 500 (SP500) fell, with Industrials losing the most. Advancers were paced by Communication Services. On the Dow (DJI), Procter & Gamble (PG) was the biggest decliner, -1.8%, while Merck (MRK) topped gainers, +1.4%. Salesforce (CRM) -0.5% before the software maker released its Q3 report late Tuesday.

Elsewhere, ZJK Industrial (ZJK) +128% after the company announced a significant advancement in its collaboration with Nvidia (NVDA) focused on advanced liquid cooling systems.

In currencies, South Korea's won (KRW:USD) pared sharper losses against the U.S. dollar (USD:KRW) after South Korean lawmakers voted to block South Korean President Yoon Suk Yeol's declaration of martial law.

data.title: PublicSquare stock triples in value after naming Donald Trump Jr. and Willie Langston to board
data.description:
Shares of PSQ Holdings (NYSE:PSQH) nearly tripled in value on Tuesday after announcing that the company has named Donald Trump Jr. and Willie Langston as members of the board of directors.

The company, d/b/a PublicSquare, said Donald Trump Jr. has been an investor in it since before its IPO, and Langston had invested in its most recent PIPE offering in October 2024.

The company's stock has a "buy" from Wall Street and "hold" from Seeking Alpha Quant.

If session gains hold, PSQH would wipe out all losses so far this year.

PSQH is currently +170% at $5.56 in late afternoon trading on the NYSE.

Press Release.

data.title: CenterPoint raised to Buy at UBS as company turns page after hurricane
data.description:
CenterPoint Energy (NYSE:CNP) +1.5% in Tuesday's trading as UBS upgrades to Buy from Neutral with a $37 price target, raised from $31, believing shares are at an inflection point where regulatory risks are moderating and a path forward is emerging on dealing with the mobile generation and Houston rate case issues.

Following a contentious debate around the rate case withdrawal application, it appears the parties may be able to settle, UBS analyst William Appicelli says, adding that if CenterPoint (CNP) is able to file a settlement, it could resolve a major hurdle in de-risking the story.

Another development that appears to be gaining momentum is a plan with mobile generation assets to be reallocated to San Antonio in the coming weeks, a potential solution that would remove the contention around CenterPoint's (CNP) ownership and utilization of the assets, the analyst says.

"At roughly an in-line multiple to the peer group average on 2026 earnings, UBS sees the stock at an attractive entry point and believe it is pricing in EPS growth at the low end of the 6%-8% stated range," Appicelli writes, believing a higher multiple is warranted given his view of strong rate base growth of ~10% and EPS growth of 7.5% underpinned by the strong growth backdrop in the Houston metro area.

data.title: BeiGene started at overweight at Morgan Stanley on Brukinsa market share growth
data.description:
• Morgan Stanley has initiated coverage of BeiGene (NASDAQ:BGNE) at overweight saying that the pharma's key drug Brukinsa (zanubrutinib) is taking over a larger share of the Bruton Tyrosine Kinase inhibitors (BTKi) market.
  • The investment bank set a $300 price target (~40% upside based on Dec. 2 close).
  • Analyst Sean Laaman said that the second-generation BTKi Brukinsa has better efficacy and safety compared to other BTKi drugs and the market for those meds are growing. For Brukinsa, he sees five-year CAGR (2023-2028E) of better than 30% to ~$6B.
  • For the BTKi market as a whole, he sees 21% growth in 2024E to ~$11B and double-digit CAGR to ~$16B in 2028E.
  • Laaman added that BeiGene has other pipeline assets that could further help the company. He highighted the BTK degrader BGB-16683 and the BCL2 inhibitor sonrotoclax, with the latter potentially in combination with Brukinsa.
  • He noted that Eli Lilly's (LLY) third-generation BTKi Jaypirca (pirtobrutinib), which currently has a third-line indication, has first-line data slated for release in Q1 2025. If the results are positive, it could potentially put pressure on Brukinsa.

data.title: Paramount's trinity of CEOs may not survive once the Skydance deal closes - report
data.description:
Paramount Global (NASDAQ:PARA) is expected to undergo sweeping changes, including cuts at the company’s TV networks, allocation of billions more for streaming, and a rejig of the top brass, Bloomberg reported Tuesday, citing people familiar with David Ellison's plans.

Ellison is exploring combining all of Paramount’s TV networks, including CBS and MTV, into one unit, the report said. Those businesses are mostly run by co-CEOs, Chris McCarthy and George Cheeks.

While Cheeks is expected to stay, McCarthy’s future is less certain, the report said. The third co-CEO, Brian Robbins, overseeing Paramount Pictures and Nickelodeon, is expected to leave around the close of the Paramount-Skydance deal, sources told Bloomberg, who asked to not be identified discussing plans that are still being formed.

The report said Ellison and Robbins, who have worked together on several movie titles, are said to have both conceded it’s unlikely Robbins will stick around, but no final decision has been made so far.

Ellison has discussed putting Dana Goldberg, the head of production at Skydance, in charge of the film business, at least for the time being, the report said. He is expected to take charge at the helm of the combined company after the deal closes next year.

data.title: Fed Governor Kugler says she’s taking ‘meeting by meeting’ approach in policy decisions
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kugler said Tuesday her decisions about monetary policy will take place “meeting by meeting” as she watches for any changes that may dent the central bank’s progress in curbing inflation.

Kugler, a voter on the Federal Open Market Committee this year, said inflation appears to be on a sustainable path to its 2% target, “even if there have been some bumps along the way,” she said in a speech at an event hosted by the Detroit Economic Club. The labor market remains “solid,” she said.

In assessing incoming data, Kugler expects to see “some bounce back” in the November U.S. nonfarm jobs report due on Friday. The October U.S. nonfarm payrolls released last month showed growth of only +12K jobs, much less than the +125K that was expected.

October’s report “should have shown something much stronger,” but the data took at hit of 100K-120K jobs because of Hurricanes Helene and Milton and a labor strike at Boeing (BA), she said during the Q&A portion of the speech.

Kugler has voted this year to cut the fed funds rate by 75 basis points to the current 4.50%-4.75% range. “I see the Fed's dual-mandate goals of maximum employment and price stability as being roughly in balance,” and policymakers are working to move monetary policy toward a more neutral setting, she said.

“Looking ahead, it is important to emphasize that policy is not on a preset course. I will make decisions meeting by meeting and carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks,” she said. With that, Kugler said she “will vigilantly monitor for incoming risks or negative supply shocks that may undo the progress that we have achieved in reducing inflation.”

The economy has strengthened this year above widely held expectations at the start of 2024, with an increase in immigration and a surge in productivity serving as key drivers, Kugler said.

“We may be getting close” to the economy reaching full employment, she said. “The question is whether we can continue on that,” Kugler said, adding that policymakers will watch "for potential negative supply shocks, potential reversals on immigration, and also maybe changes in trade."

Kugler said it is "too early” to make judgements on immigration policy proposed by the incoming Trump administration. “Studying the specifics, when they come out, will be important, as trade policy may affect productivity and prices,” she said.

Separately, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said Tuesday that he expects interest rates to continue falling in the next year.

data.title: Nebius surges as Citron pounds the table, says CEO is 'the real deal'
data.description:
Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) shares surged more than 8% on Tuesday as investment firm Citron Research pounded the table on the artificial intelligence-focused technology firm and said CEO Arkady Volozh is "the real deal."

"Citron pounding the table that $NBIS is poised to be the next AI Wall Street darling," the research firm wrote on the X social network. "A sleeper with no analyst coverage yet, the market hasn’t caught on to its massive potential—or its undervaluation vs. CoreWeave." Shares have gained nearly 35% over the past month.

Nebius, which was once known as Yandex before it severed ties with the Russian firm, was likened to cloud service provider Coreweave by Citron, which is likely to go public next year.

"If it trades at a 50% discount to Coreweave multiple that puts $NBIS at $60 (napkin math)," Citron added, explaining that Nvidia's (NVDA) investment into Nebius was "not out of the blue."

On Monday, Nvidia was among the backers in a $700M private placement financing round by Nebius. Other investors include Accel and certain accounts managed by Orbis Investments. The financing will help Nebius' plans to further build out its full-stack AI infrastructure.

data.title: Chicago Fed's Goolsbee sees rates falling in next year; keeps eye on housing inflation
data.description:
"Over the next year, it feels to me like rates will come down from where we are now," Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said Tuesday during a Q&A. "My belief that rates will and should come down" is based on the data, he added.

The reduction in inflation without sparking a recession was achieved through the healing of supply-side issues.

Monthly payroll employment, a great indicator of the economy, has "broken down a little bit," Goolsbee said. "The inflation series is kind of my pet metric." Specifically, he at housing inflation closely, which is down from its peak, but still higher than it was before the pandemic.

2023 was a "hall of fame year for inflation falling," and marked the only time that happened without triggering a recession. A huge decline in inflation without a recession "is a triumph," he said.

People are still feeling that price levels are higher than they were before the pandemic, he admits. But the Fed's job isn't to lower price levels, but to reduce inflation. The only way to produce deflation — or lowering price levels — is to tank the economy, Goolsbee added.

Breaking news... check back for updates.
